patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 29-year-old anxious but clear-speaking elementary school teacher. Open with: “I’ve had a rash and low fever since I got back from my trip, and my eyes feel really irritated.” Volunteer: itchy red rash that began on your face then spread to chest/arms over a day, low-grade fever/chills, red gritty “sand” eyes, aching wrists/ankles, headache, and feeling wiped out. If asked, add: rash started ~2 days after returning from a tropical trip with many mosquito bites, mild muscle aches, pain behind the eyes with movement, occasional blurry vision worse in bright light, and you don’t see the usual central “shine” in your eye. Do not volunteer unless directly asked: dark urine from poor intake, small tender neck lumps, frequent ibuprofen use, and that you and your partner were trying to get pregnant. Act embarrassed and initially avoid mentioning unprotected sex while traveling and mild burning with urination; disclose only if asked about sexual history/urinary symptoms. Express misconceptions: worry it’s dengue or chikungunya; wonder if a new “immunity” herbal supplement caused an allergy. If the clinician is empathetic, you calm slightly, thank them, and ask about costs/insurance and whether tests are truly necessary.

medications: voriconazole 200 mg PO every 12 hours
acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for fever/pain
confounders: Recent travel to mosquito-endemic region with concurrent dengue exposure risk
Equivocal dengue IgM due to flavivirus serologic cross-reactivity
Voriconazole therapy for fusariosis with potential hepatotoxicity complicating interpretation of mild transaminitis
Ocular finding (absent foveal reflex) suggesting retinal involvement that can overlap with other infectious or inflammatory retinopathies

correct_diagnosis.primary: Zika virus infectious disease
required_evidence: Epidemiologic risk (recent travel to or residence in Zika-affected area or sexual exposure to a potentially exposed partner) with compatible acute illness OR pregnancy with exposure
Positive Zika virus NAAT (RT-PCR) in serum and/or urine collected in the appropriate time window after symptom onset/exposure

Clinical compatibility (often mild febrile illness with rash, arthralgia, conjunctivitis) recognizing that symptoms may be minimal/absent; ocular finding noted as absent foveal reflex suggests retinal involvement requiring ophthalmology evaluation
Exclusion/consideration of key alternative arboviral infections (e.g., dengue, chikungunya) based on clinical course and available testing (not provided here)

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Often normal or mild leukopenia; may be within reference range
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Typically normal or mildly low; marked thrombocytopenia is less typical than dengue
expected_lab_results.Zika virus NAAT (RT-PCR) - Serum: May be positive early (generally within ~7 days of symptom onset); negative does not exclude if outside window
expected_lab_results.Zika virus NAAT (RT-PCR) - Urine: May be positive slightly longer than serum (often up to ~14 days after symptom onset)

medications: Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain/fever (max 3000 mg/day; lower max if liver disease or heavy alcohol use)
non_pharmacological: Supportive care: rest, oral hydration
Avoid NSAIDs and aspirin until dengue is reasonably excluded due to bleeding risk
Prevent mosquito exposure during the first week of illness (repellent, long sleeves, bed nets) to reduce onward transmission

Sexual transmission prevention: use condoms or abstain; counsel on recommended duration (men: at least 3 months after symptom onset/exposure; women: at least 2 months) and longer/individualized counseling if pregnancy is involved

Ophthalmology referral/urgent eye evaluation for abnormal macular finding (absent foveal reflex) to assess for retinal/macular involvement and document visual acuity; advise prompt evaluation if vision changes occur
If pregnant or trying to conceive: coordinate obstetric care; consider serial fetal ultrasounds and appropriate maternal-fetal medicine follow-up per local guidance
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 48-72 hours (sooner if worsening symptoms), and ensure ophthalmology follow-up within 1 week or urgently if any visual symptoms; additional follow-up based on pregnancy status and test results

must_include: Zika is a viral infection usually causing mild illness; some people have few or no symptoms
How diagnosis is supported by RT-PCR testing and its timing limits (a negative result may occur if tested outside the detection window)
Key complications to watch for: pregnancy-related fetal risks; rare neurologic complications (e.g., Guillain-Barré); ocular involvement requiring eye evaluation
No specific antiviral therapy; treatment is supportive
Use acetaminophen for fever/pain; avoid NSAIDs/aspirin until dengue is excluded
Hydration and rest; when to seek urgent care (worsening symptoms, bleeding, severe headache/neck stiffness, neurologic symptoms, vision changes)
Discuss pregnancy status and reproductive plans; offer counseling/testing pathway if pregnant
Explain sexual transmission risk and recommended condom/abstinence durations
Explain mosquito-bite prevention to protect others
Mosquito avoidance measures (EPA-registered repellent, protective clothing, screens/bed nets)
Avoid donating blood/semen for the recommended period after exposure/illness
Follow-up plan and importance of ophthalmology evaluation for the abnormal eye finding

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for Zika evaluation: symptom onset and timeline, travel/residence in endemic area, mosquito exposure, sexual exposure, pregnancy status/reproductive plans, red flags (bleeding, severe headache/neck stiffness, neuro symptoms, vision changes), and comorbidities/medications.

Order appropriate diagnostics based on timing: Zika NAAT/RT-PCR (serum and urine within detection window), consider Zika IgM if outside NAAT window, and order dengue testing to guide safe analgesic choice; consider pregnancy test if status uncertain.
Interpret results with attention to timing limitations and cross-reactivity; use results to support diagnosis and rule out dengue before recommending NSAIDs/aspirin.
Make or update diagnosis (confirmed or suspected Zika) based on clinical picture plus lab results and exposure history; document differential (dengue/chikungunya) as appropriate.
Critical safety check before any prescribing (e.g., acetaminophen) to avoid allergic reactions.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing to identify potential interactions with current medications (e.g., acetaminophen with other combination products, hepatotoxic agents).
Provide supportive treatment (e.g., acetaminophen) and explicitly avoid NSAIDs/aspirin until dengue is excluded; include hydration/rest recommendations.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and test timing limits; explain supportive treatment and urgent return precautions; address pregnancy/sexual transmission and mosquito prevention; counsel on blood/semen donation avoidance; emphasize ophthalmology evaluation for eye findings.
Arrange follow-up, including ophthalmology evaluation for ocular involvement and OB/MFM pathway if pregnant; provide timeline for reassessment and test result review.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding of plan, precautions, and follow-up.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 46-year-old woman who is anxious and talks in a tangential, story-like way. Start with: burning when you pee, needing to go every few minutes with little output, and lower belly cramps. If asked, add: strong-smelling urine, urgency, cloudy urine, on-and-off chills without checking a big fever, fatigue, and similar episodes 1–2 times in the past year.

Assume it’s “just another bladder infection” from not drinking water at work. Mention you tried cranberry juice/supplements and took a friend’s leftover antibiotics for 2 days then stopped when you felt a bit better. Say your back is sore but blame lifting boxes.

Do NOT volunteer at first: OTC UTI relief pills turning urine orange, drinking less water, occasional back-to-front wiping, constipation/straining, new scented body wash.

Strongly resist disclosing: new unprotected sex partner, unusual discharge/odor, painful sex; if asked directly and reassured about confidentiality/costs, admit reluctantly and worry about STI stigma/records.

If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, ask for a quick affordable fix, and express fear of costly tests, missing work, and insurance consequences; doubt generics but worry about price.

acceptable_alternatives: Anemia of chronic inflammation (anemia of chronic disease)
required_evidence: CBC shows microcytic anemia (low MCV) with elevated RDW

No alternative explanation on CBC pattern (e.g., very low MCV with normal RDW suggesting thalassemia trait)

Dietary counseling to increase iron intake (heme iron sources; iron-fortified foods)
Avoid taking iron with calcium, antacids, tea/coffee; separate by at least 2 hours

must_include: You have anemia due to low iron, which leads to smaller red blood cells

Discuss red flags requiring urgent evaluation (chest pain, syncope, severe shortness of breath, black/tarry stools, heavy ongoing bleeding)

Avoid tea/coffee around iron-containing meals

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms, duration, severity, and functional impact; screen for red flags (chest pain, syncope, severe dyspnea) and bleeding (melena, hematochezia, heavy menses).
Clarify risk factors/etiologies for microcytic anemia: dietary intake, pica, pregnancy/postpartum, GI symptoms, NSAID use, prior bariatric surgery/celiac symptoms, family history of thalassemia.
Obtain current medication list and supplements (antacids/PPIs, calcium, levothyroxine, tetracyclines/fluoroquinolones) and document allergies (penicillin history) for safe prescribing and counseling.

Order diagnostic labs to confirm iron deficiency anemia and assess severity: CBC with indices/RDW, reticulocyte count, ferritin, serum iron, TIBC/transferrin saturation; consider CMP and pregnancy test if relevant.
Interpret results to establish the characteristic pattern (low Hgb/Hct, low MCV, low iron with high TIBC, low ferritin) and rule out alternative causes needing different management.
State diagnosis as iron deficiency anemia (microcytic anemia) and note need to identify underlying cause (bleeding vs intake vs absorption).
Perform allergy check before prescribing; note penicillin allergy is not relevant to oral iron but should remain documented.

Critical safety check: identify interacting meds/supplements that reduce iron absorption or are affected by iron (e.g., levothyroxine, tetracyclines/fluoroquinolones, calcium, antacids/PPIs) and plan spacing/alternatives.
Initiate first-line oral iron therapy with appropriate dosing strategy (often every-other-day elemental iron) and clear administration instructions.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and lab pattern; explain treatment timeline and administration; address red flags and interaction avoidance; provide lifestyle/diet counseling and adherence expectations.

Arrange reassessment (e.g., repeat CBC in ~2–4 weeks to confirm response; ferritin later to confirm repletion) and plan evaluation for source of iron loss (especially GI bleeding evaluation when indicated).
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

diagnosis.primary: congenital myasthenic syndromes with glycosylation defect

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital myasthenic syndrome (CMS) due to glycosylation pathway defect (e.g., GMPPB-related CMS)
acceptable_alternatives: Congenital myasthenic syndrome due to glycosylation defect
required_evidence: History of chronic fluctuating fatigable weakness beginning in infancy/childhood (often limb-girdle and/or bulbar) without autoimmune features
Worsening with exertion and improvement with rest; possible ptosis/ophthalmoparesis but may be mild/variable
Electrodiagnostic evidence of neuromuscular junction transmission defect (decrement on low-frequency RNS and/or increased jitter on SFEMG)
Lack of AChR/MuSK antibody-mediated myasthenia features (seronegative autoimmune MG or low clinical suspicion for autoimmune MG)
Genetic confirmation of a glycosylation-pathway CMS variant (e.g., GMPPB, GFPT1, DPAGT1) or strong phenotype consistent with this group
No alternative explanation such as primary myopathy, motor neuron disease, or metabolic/electrolyte cause

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no anemia expected to explain weakness)
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - Bicarbon: Typically normal (may decrease if acute respiratory insufficiency with compensation, but not expected at baseline)

medications: Pyridostigmine 30 mg PO every 6 hours while awake (titrate to effect and tolerability; typical max 60 mg PO every 4-6 hours)
Albuterol (salbutamol) 2 mg PO three times daily (or 4 mg PO twice daily) as adjunct if inadequate response to pyridostigmine; monitor heart rate and potassium
3,4-Diaminopyridine (amifampridine) 10 mg PO three times daily (titrate; max 80 mg/day) as specialist-guided option depending on genotype/response
non_pharmacological: Neurology/neuromuscular specialist referral for genotype-directed therapy and confirmation (RNS/SFEMG and genetic testing if not already done)

Avoid medications known to worsen neuromuscular transmission (especially macrolides, fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides, magnesium, certain beta-blockers) unless no alternatives and with close monitoring
Respiratory assessment plan: teach recognition of respiratory decline; consider baseline pulmonary function (FVC/NIF) when available and urgent evaluation for dyspnea
Physical therapy/energy conservation strategies; pacing and rest breaks to reduce fatigability
Swallow safety assessment if bulbar symptoms present; aspiration precautions as needed
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks after initiating or adjusting therapy, sooner (same day/ED) for worsening shortness of breath, dysphagia, or rapidly progressive weakness

must_include: CMS is a genetic neuromuscular junction disorder causing fatigable weakness (not autoimmune myasthenia)
Symptoms can fluctuate and worsen with exertion/illness; some antibiotics and other drugs can worsen weakness
Diagnosis is supported by EMG testing and confirmed/clarified with genetic testing; subtype matters for best treatment
Purpose and expected benefits of pyridostigmine (improves neuromuscular transmission; symptom relief)
How to take medication on schedule; titration plan and what side effects to watch for (cramps, diarrhea, increased secretions)
Adjunct options (albuterol and/or amifampridine) may be considered by neuromuscular specialist; monitoring needs (tachycardia, tremor; seizure risk with amifampridine at high doses)
Review allergy history: azithromycin caused worsened weakness/shortness of breath; penicillin caused urticaria—avoid and document clearly
Red flags requiring urgent care: increasing shortness of breath, trouble swallowing, inability to handle secretions, rapidly worsening weakness
Plan for infections: contact clinician before starting new antibiotics; avoid macrolides when possible
Avoid known triggers (overexertion, sleep deprivation, intercurrent illness) and pace activities
Medication avoidance list and to inform all clinicians/pharmacists about CMS and allergies
Importance of follow-up with neuromuscular specialist and adherence to therapy/monitoring

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old male electrician. You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. Start by saying you get brutal one-sided headaches that hit out of nowhere, usually at night. Volunteer: severe stabbing pain behind the left eye, peaks within minutes, so intense you can’t sit still (pace/rock), occurs in clusters for a few weeks then months of no headaches, left eye gets red and waters during attacks.

Withhold unless specifically asked about safety/substances: occasional scary thoughts (“can’t do this anymore”) without plan/intent; you took a friend’s strong pain pills a couple times. Also mention only if asked: normal between attacks, pre-attack restlessness, same time daily, OTC meds don’t help, you smoke more during clusters.

Express misconceptions: think it’s sinus/eye/migraine; antibiotics didn’t help. Raise cost/job concerns about oxygen, meds causing drowsiness, neurologist/scans. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, admit fear, and ask for practical, affordable options.

Verapamil immediate-release 80 mg PO three times daily

Family history of migraine in a first-degree relative may blur distinction between migraine and cluster headache
Possible obstructive sleep apnea risk (overweight, smoker) which is associated with headache complaints and sleep disruption

correct_diagnosis.primary: Cluster headache (familial)
required_evidence: Recurrent attacks of severe or very severe strictly unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes

At least one ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptom during attacks (conjunctival injection/lacrimation, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, eyelid edema, facial sweating, miosis/ptosis) and/or sense of restlessness/agitation
Clustered pattern (bouts over weeks–months with remissions) and/or strong circadian timing
No red-flag features suggesting secondary headache (e.g., focal neurologic deficits, meningismus, thunderclap onset, persistent new headache pattern, systemic symptoms)
Family history consistent with familial cluster headache (supports but does not replace clinical criteria)

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 28-year-old female elementary school teacher. Be cooperative and clear. Start by saying you’ve felt wiped out for weeks, get winded climbing stairs, and your eyes look yellow on and off. Volunteer: dark urine sometimes (worse in mornings) and a dull/full feeling under the left ribs. If asked about other symptoms, mention headaches/lightheadedness on standing, racing heart with exertion, past similar episodes after colds/stomach bugs, easier bruising, and looking pale. Do NOT mention unless specifically asked: prior gallbladder-type attacks after greasy food/possible gallstones, being told your spleen was big, family member with a “blood condition”/intermittent anemia, and that you only take folic acid inconsistently. If asked about meds/supplements, admit you started iron because you assumed low iron; say it hasn’t helped. Express the misconception that yellow eyes might be from food or occasional drinking (you rarely drink). If asked about sexual symptoms, deny erectile issues (that’s not applicable). If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and say you’re anxious about costs, insurance coverage, missed work, and frequent monitoring.

Peripheral smear.value: Stomatocytes 15-20% of RBCs; polychromasia present; no schistocytes
Peripheral smear.normal_range: No significant stomatocytosis; normal RBC morphology

medications: Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
Drospirenone/ethinyl estradiol 3 mg/0.03 mg PO daily
Potassium chloride 20 mEq PO daily
comorbidities: Iron overload due to chronic hemolysis (no transfusion history)
confounders: Recent viral upper respiratory infection 2 weeks ago with worsened fatigue (can mimic hemolytic flare)
Combined oral contraceptive use (increases baseline VTE risk; some hereditary stomatocytosis variants have thrombosis risk, especially post-splenectomy)
Iron studies elevated, which may be misattributed to hereditary hemochromatosis or inflammation rather than hemolysis-related iron loading
Intermittent mild hypokalemia (may suggest renal/GI loss and distract from primary hemolytic process)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hereditary stomatocytosis (autosomal dominant hemolytic anemia due to red cell membrane permeability defect)
required_evidence: CBC pattern consistent with chronic hemolytic anemia (low hemoglobin/hematocrit with compensatory marrow response suggested clinically)
MCHC characteristically decreased in overhydrated hereditary stomatocytosis OR increased/normal-high in dehydrated hereditary stomatocytosis (xerocytosis)
MCV often increased (macrocytosis), especially in overhydrated hereditary stomatocytosis
RDW may be elevated due to variable red cell morphology
Clinical history consistent with lifelong or familial hemolysis (e.g., intermittent jaundice, anemia, gallstones, splenomegaly) even if not provided explicitly
Exclusion of more common causes of hemolytic anemia when appropriate (e.g., autoimmune hemolysis, hereditary spherocytosis)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Decreased (mild to moderate anemia, variable by phenotype)
expected_lab_results.CBC - RBC count: Decreased or inappropriately normal for degree of anemia
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCHC: Decreased in overhydrated hereditary stomatocytosis; normal-high/increased in dehydrated hereditary stomatocytosis (xerocytosis)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Usually normal (may be elevated post-splenectomy if performed historically)

medications: Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
non_pharmacological: Avoid splenectomy unless directed by hematology (increased thrombotic risk in hereditary stomatocytosis, particularly xerocytosis)
Hematology referral for confirmatory testing and subtype classification (peripheral smear for stomatocytes, hemolysis labs and specialized testing/genetics as indicated)
Assess and manage complications of chronic hemolysis: gallstones (symptom review and imaging if symptomatic), iron overload risk if transfused, thrombotic risk counseling especially if splenectomized
Vaccination review if asplenic or if splenectomy ever considered (pneumococcal, meningococcal, Hib) per guidelines
Transfusion support only if symptomatic anemia or acute exacerbation (coordinate with hematology)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 2-4 weeks; sooner (24-72 hours) if symptomatic anemia, jaundice, dark urine, or worsening fatigue

must_include: This is an inherited red blood cell membrane condition that can cause chronic hemolytic anemia
It is not an infection and is not contagious
Some subtypes have higher clot risk, and splenectomy is generally avoided unless a specialist recommends it
Folic acid supports red blood cell production in chronic hemolysis
Transfusions are reserved for significant symptoms or severe anemia
Specialist testing is needed to confirm subtype and guide long-term management
Review warning signs that need urgent care (chest pain, shortness of breath, fainting, severe fatigue, new jaundice/dark urine, signs of clot such as leg swelling/pain)
Discuss family implications and that relatives may benefit from evaluation if there is a family history
Maintain hydration and avoid triggers that worsen anemia when applicable (e.g., intercurrent illness—seek early care)
Avoid iron supplements unless iron deficiency is confirmed (iron overload can occur if transfused)
Keep vaccinations up to date, especially if spleen function is reduced or splenectomy is ever considered

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history: anemia/hemolysis symptoms, triggers, prior transfusions, family history, prior splenectomy or thrombotic events, medications/supplements (iron), pregnancy status if relevant, and red flags needing urgent care.
Order baseline evaluation of hemolysis/anemia and to support/triage suspected hereditary stomatocytosis and rule out common mimics.

Interpret results for chronic hemolysis pattern (e.g., anemia with reticulocytosis, elevated bilirubin/LDH, low haptoglobin) and review smear indices; decide if stable vs needs urgent escalation/transfusion.
State working diagnosis of hereditary stomatocytosis (or suspected) and acknowledge need for specialist confirmatory testing/subtyping; avoid definitive subtype claims without confirmatory tests.

Explain diagnosis, non-contagious nature, clot/splenectomy considerations, treatment principles (folate, transfusion thresholds), lifestyle guidance, and family implications; provide red-flag return precautions.
Mandatory critical safety check before any prescribing.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing to identify interactions (e.g., with folic acid or any other planned meds).
If appropriate, prescribe folic acid supplementation for chronic hemolysis; avoid iron unless deficiency is confirmed; avoid recommending splenectomy.
Arrange hematology referral and follow-up for confirmatory testing/subtype guidance, monitoring anemia/hemolysis, and counseling (including family screening).
Close encounter after summarizing plan, ensuring understanding, and confirming safety-net instructions.

volunteer: Nighttime attacks that start out of nowhere, sometimes several times a night
My partner says I sit up suddenly, thrash, kick, or make strange movements for less than a minute
I’m tired during the day because my sleep feels broken
if_asked: The episodes usually happen soon after I fall asleep, not in the morning
I’m often aware right after it happens and can remember parts of it, like a sudden fear or a jolt
Sometimes I make noises like grunting or shouting during the episode
I don’t usually lose control of my bladder during these episodes
I’ve had similar events since my teens, and they’ve come and gone over the years
Someone in my family has had similar night episodes or ‘seizures’

I’ve had a few small injuries from falling out of bed or hitting the nightstand
My partner says I can look awake during it, but I’m not really responding normally

I’ve had a few episodes where I shouted swear words or made sexual-sounding noises in my sleep, which is really embarrassing

misleading: I’ve gained weight easily since I was a kid and always feel hungry, so I wondered if this is some kind of hormone or genetic syndrome

Occasional mild headaches in the afternoon
My knees ache after long walks

general_concerns: Because the episodes only happen at night, it must be a sleep disorder like night terrors, not epilepsy.
If I don’t lose consciousness or have full-body shaking, it can’t be a real seizure.
My smartwatch sleep tracker would show it if I was having seizures, so it’s probably anxiety or stress.
If I can remember parts of it, it means I was awake and just panicking.
Since my dad had similar ‘sleep attacks,’ it’s just genetic and there’s nothing treatment can really change.
Anti-seizure medication will change my personality or make me ‘foggy’ permanently, so it’s safer to avoid it unless things get worse.
If I stop caffeine and alcohol completely, the episodes should go away without medication.
economic_concerns: I’m worried an epilepsy diagnosis will make me lose my job or prevent me from working nights.
I can’t afford repeated sleep studies, EEGs, or genetic testing if insurance doesn’t cover them.
I’m concerned the medication cost will be ongoing and I’ll need frequent specialist visits I can’t easily take time off for.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 26-year-old anxious but clear-speaking male IT support specialist on night shift. Start with: “I keep having weird episodes in my sleep where I jerk awake and move like I’m fighting or running.” Volunteer: sudden nighttime attacks, sometimes several times/night; partner sees you sit up, thrash/kick/odd movements <1 minute; you’re tired because sleep feels broken. If asked, add: usually soon after falling asleep (not morning); you’re aware right after and remember fear/jolt; grunting/shouting; no usual bladder loss; since teens, comes and goes; family history of similar episodes/seizures. Only if specifically probed about aura/injuries/day impact: brief warning (rush of fear/weird stomach), irritability and poor focus after bad nights, minor injuries, look awake but not responding normally. Resist/withhold unless directly asked in a nonjudgmental way: cheek/tongue biting, swearing/sexual-sounding noises, avoiding travel/sleepovers. Express misconceptions: wonder about hormones/genetics due to weight/hunger; think snoring/sleep apnea or night terrors. Emphasize cost/job fears about epilepsy, testing, meds, time off. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and share withheld details.

Emollient cream topical PRN xerosis
comorbidities: Autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy (sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy)

confounders: Parasomnias (sleepwalking/night terrors) mimicking nocturnal seizures

diagnosis.primary: autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy
differentials: Prader-Willi-like syndrome due to point mutation
Prader-Willi syndrome due to point mutation

correct_diagnosis.primary: Autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy (ADNFLE) / sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy (familial)
required_evidence: Recurrent, brief, stereotyped nocturnal motor events (hypermotor/complex movements) arising from sleep with abrupt onset/offset
Episodes cluster during the night and recur over weeks to months
Features favor seizure over parasomnia: high stereotypy, multiple events per night, occurrence across sleep stages, possible dystonic posturing/vocalization
Supportive history: family history consistent with autosomal dominant pattern OR known pathogenic variant associated with ADNFLE (e.g., CHRNA4/CHRNB2/CHRNA2, KCNT1)

Exclusion of Prader-Willi phenotype: no consistent hyperphagia/obesity with typical PWS endocrine features; clinical picture primarily nocturnal seizures rather than syndromic hypotonia/developmental/endocrine presentation
If available: video-EEG or polysomnography with extended EEG showing ictal/interictal frontal epileptiform activity (note: may be normal; diagnosis can be clinical when typical)

expected_lab_results.CBC (WBC): Typically normal; no leukocytosis expected in uncomplicated epilepsy
expected_lab_results.CMP (Sodium): Typically normal (hyponatremia would raise concern for medication effect such as carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine if already on therapy)
expected_lab_results.CMP (Creatinine): Typically normal (used to guide dosing if renal impairment present)

medications: Carbamazepine 100-200 mg PO at bedtime, titrate by 100-200 mg every 3-7 days to 400-800 mg/day in divided doses (or bedtime-focused dosing if purely nocturnal) as tolerated
non_pharmacological: Seizure safety counseling: avoid sleep deprivation, alcohol excess; ensure safe sleep environment (remove sharp objects, consider bed on low height, supervision if needed)
Driving/work safety counseling per local regulations; avoid operating dangerous machinery until seizures controlled and legally cleared
Arrange diagnostic confirmation when feasible: overnight video-EEG or polysomnography with extended EEG montage; consider brain MRI if not previously done or if atypical features
Consider genetic counseling/testing if strong family history or early onset; discuss implications for relatives
If inadequate response or intolerant: plan alternative antiseizure medication trial (e.g., oxcarbazepine, lacosamide, or other focal-seizure agents guided by specialist)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up in 4-6 weeks (earlier in 1-2 weeks if frequent events, adverse effects, or dose titration issues).

must_include: Events are most consistent with sleep-related focal seizures from the frontal lobe (often brief, stereotyped, and occurring in clusters at night)
This condition can run in families (autosomal dominant pattern) and is different from parasomnias/night terrors
Differentials (including Prader-Willi-related syndromes) are less likely when the primary issue is stereotyped nocturnal hypermotor spells without the characteristic syndromic features
Goal is seizure reduction/elimination and improved sleep safety
Carbamazepine is a first-line option for focal seizures and often effective in nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy; dosing will be titrated
Common side effects (dizziness, drowsiness, nausea, rash) and serious warning signs (rash with mucosal involvement, fever, facial swelling) requiring urgent evaluation
Need for monitoring plan (clinical response; consider sodium monitoring if symptoms of hyponatremia or if using oxcarbazepine/carbamazepine; follow clinician guidance)
Discuss safety at night and risk of injury; create a practical home plan
Discuss reproductive/teratogenic counseling if applicable and contraception interactions (enzyme induction with carbamazepine can reduce hormonal contraceptive efficacy)
Clarify when to seek emergency care (prolonged seizure, repeated seizures without recovery, injury, new focal deficits, severe allergic reaction)
Prioritize regular sleep schedule and avoid sleep deprivation
Limit alcohol and avoid recreational drugs that can lower seizure threshold
Medication adherence and not stopping antiseizure medication abruptly
Driving and activity restrictions until controlled and per legal requirements

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history to distinguish sleep-related focal seizures (stereotyped nocturnal hypermotor events) from parasomnias and to assess seizure burden, triggers, injuries, and red flags.
Clarify family history (autosomal dominant pattern), developmental/syndromic features (to make Prader-Willi-related syndromes less likely), and medication/substance use relevant to seizure threshold.
Order baseline safety labs relevant to antiseizure therapy and comorbidity screening (e.g., CBC, CMP/LFTs, sodium) and pregnancy test if applicable.
Review labs to identify contraindications/precautions (hepatic dysfunction, hyponatremia risk, hematologic abnormalities) before selecting and dosing medication.

Document working diagnosis consistent with autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy (sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy), and note key differentials considered (parasomnias/night terrors, other focal epilepsies, syndromic causes) and why less likely.
Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm allergies (including prior rash/SCAR to antiseizure meds) and relevant risk history.

Critical safety check before prescribing: evaluate drug-drug interactions (enzyme induction with carbamazepine; reduced hormonal contraceptive efficacy; interactions with anticoagulants, antiretrovirals, etc.).

Perform contraindication screening prior to prescribing (pregnancy/trying to conceive, hepatic disease, history of severe cutaneous reactions; assess appropriateness of carbamazepine vs alternatives).
Initiate first-line therapy for focal seizures (carbamazepine) with titration plan, tailored to patient factors and after safety checks.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis vs parasomnias; explain treatment goals, titration, side effects and serious warning signs; provide safety plan, emergency precautions, reproductive counseling/contraception interactions, lifestyle guidance, and driving/activity restrictions.
Arrange follow-up to assess seizure control, tolerability, adherence, and need for monitoring (including sodium if symptomatic or per clinician guidance) and to adjust dosing.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, questions answered, and clear return precautions.

acceptable_alternatives: CMS due to defects in N-linked glycosylation pathway (e.g., GFPT1-related CMS, DPAGT1-related CMS)

No autoimmune myasthenia features (e.g., negative AChR/MuSK antibodies if available) and no thymoma history
Electrophysiology supportive of neuromuscular junction disorder (decrement on low-frequency repetitive nerve stimulation and/or increased jitter on single-fiber EMG)
Genetic confirmation of a CMS glycosylation-pathway gene (e.g., GMPPB, GFPT1, DPAGT1) or strong clinical-genetic suspicion pending results
Clinical response pattern consistent with CMS (often partial response to acetylcholinesterase inhibitors and/or adrenergic agonists depending on subtype)

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal (no leukocytosis expected unless intercurrent infection)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): Normal to mildly elevated if chronic hypoventilation; low if acute respiratory compromise with metabolic compensation not yet established

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 46-year-old anxious, tangential project manager. Start with: “I can’t sleep at night, and it’s starting to mess with everything.” Volunteering early: you lie awake for hours, wake multiple times, feel foggy/worn out next day, happening most nights for ~2 months. Ramble about work stress, deadlines, and how it’s affecting performance.

If asked, share: racing thoughts/worry at bedtime, early awakenings, irritability, more afternoon coffee, phone use in bed, occasional sweaty/shaky awakenings, more nighttime urination.

Do NOT mention unless specifically probed (or late in visit): morning headaches/dry mouth, snoring and “stopping breathing then gasping,” weight gain, nighttime heartburn, jumpy legs needing movement, unrefreshing light sleep, variable morning blood sugars.

Be resistant/guarded about: using alcohol to “knock yourself out,” frequent OTC sleep aids, feeling down/hopeless, panic-y nighttime feelings—minimize and say you don’t want a depression label.

Express misconceptions: hormones/thyroid, low blood sugar so you eat sweets at bedtime, “adrenal fatigue.” Push for a quick, cheap prescription; worry about costs of therapy, follow-ups, and sleep studies; mention sticking with inexpensive options like methylpentynol. If clinician is empathic, briefly soften (“thanks”) but quickly return to worries and desire for a fast fix.

patient.instructions:

Start with: “I keep getting these scary headaches where half my body goes weak like I’m having a stroke.” Volunteer: one-sided throbbing headache building over ~1 hour; left arm/leg sudden weakness/clumsiness before headache; light/sound worsen; nausea. Emphasize fear of stroke.

If asked about warning signs/neurologic symptoms, add: zig-zag/shimmering vision; same-side facial tingling/numbness; brief slurred speech/word-finding trouble; weakness lasts hours then resolves; since late teens, now more frequent; mom had “migraines with paralysis”; triggers include stress, poor sleep, sometimes around period; movement worsens so you lie in a dark room.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Familial hemiplegic migraine (FHM)
acceptable_alternatives: Hemiplegic migraine (sporadic hemiplegic migraine if no family history)
Migraine with aura (if motor weakness is not confirmed)
required_evidence: Recurrent migraine attacks with aura that includes fully reversible motor weakness (hemiplegic aura)
At least one additional fully reversible aura symptom (e.g., visual, sensory, speech/language) and typical aura evolution (gradual spread over minutes) and duration consistent with migraine aura
Headache temporally associated with aura (during aura or within 60 minutes) and migraine features (e.g., throbbing, unilateral, photophobia/phonophobia, nausea)
Family history of similar attacks consistent with familial hemiplegic migraine (first- or second-degree relative), or prior confirmed diagnosis/genetic diagnosis if available

No red flags suggesting acute stroke/secondary cause (e.g., persistent deficit, sudden maximal onset, new severe headache, fever/meningismus, anticoagulation, significant vascular risk) OR documented prior workup excluding stroke when clinically indicated

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up within 2-4 weeks (earlier within 1 week if first presentation, prolonged deficits, or frequent attacks); primary care follow-up within 1-2 weeks for medication monitoring

must_include: Hemiplegic migraine is a migraine subtype where aura can include temporary one-sided weakness that is reversible
Symptoms can mimic stroke; seek emergency care if symptoms are new, unusually severe, sudden, or do not fully resolve

Acute plan focuses on non-vasoconstrictive options (NSAID +/- antiemetic) and supportive care

State working diagnosis (classical trigeminal neuralgia vs atypical/secondary facial pain) based on history and red-flag assessment.
Explain diagnosis in patient-friendly terms, distinguish from infection/dental disease, and outline red flags that warrant further evaluation.
Critical safety check before prescribing first-line agents (carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine) and alternatives.
Critical safety check before prescribing due to strong CYP interactions (especially carbamazepine) and additive CNS effects.
Order baseline labs prior to or at initiation: CBC and serum sodium (and consider LFTs per local protocol) to monitor for blood dyscrasias and hyponatremia.
Review baseline labs if available to support safe initiation and establish monitoring plan.
Initiate first-line therapy with carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine, starting low and titrating; include counseling on side effects, serious reactions, and when to stop/seek urgent care.

Provide treatment education, titration/adherence guidance, driving precautions if sedated, not stopping abruptly, trigger management, oral hygiene strategies, and nutrition/hydration plan for feeding difficulty.
Arrange timely reassessment for symptom response and adverse effects, and plan follow-up labs (CBC/sodium) after initiation/titration; discuss imaging/referral if atypical features or poor response.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent characterizes pain as brief, severe, shock-like or stabbing, typically unilateral, in trigeminal distribution, with triggers such as touch, chewing, brushing teeth, or cold air, and with pain-free intervals between attacks.

Agent explicitly screens for red flags/secondary causes: numbness or sensory loss, weakness, persistent background pain between attacks, bilateral symptoms, age <40 or atypical course, new neurologic symptoms, systemic symptoms, or concerning dental/sinus features.

Agent differentiates trigeminal neuralgia from dental infection and other facial pain (e.g., temporomandibular disorder, cluster headache, postherpetic neuralgia) and recommends dental evaluation if diagnostic uncertainty persists.

Agent explains diagnosis using required content: trigeminal nerve irritation/misfiring with triggerable attacks; not an infection and distinct from dental disease; red flags requiring further evaluation (numbness, weakness, persistent pain between attacks, bilateral symptoms, new neurologic symptoms).
Before any prescribing, agent performs all required safety checks: allergy_check, contraindication_check, and drug_interaction_check; failure to do any is a critical error.
Agent selects guideline-consistent first-line pharmacotherapy (carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine) and communicates start-low/go-slow titration and expected timeframe for benefit.

Agent counsels on common side effects (drowsiness, dizziness, nausea) and serious warnings (rash/severe skin reaction, hyponatremia, low blood counts) and gives clear urgent return instructions for rash, fever, mouth sores, confusion, severe fatigue, syncope/fainting, or severe/worsening symptoms.
Agent orders and explains need for baseline and follow-up labs (at minimum CBC and sodium) and links monitoring to the chosen medication and titration plan.

Agent addresses feeding difficulty with a short-term intake plan (soft foods, small frequent meals, hydration, timing analgesia/medication before meals when appropriate) and assesses dehydration risk.
Agent provides safety-net advice on when to seek urgent care: inability to maintain hydration, uncontrolled pain, new neurologic deficits, or suspected medication reaction.
Agent includes lifestyle counseling: identify/minimize triggers, gentle oral hygiene, avoid driving/operating machinery if sedated, do not stop anticonvulsant abruptly, and keep a pain/trigger diary.

Agent uses shared decision-making regarding MRI/imaging and neurology/neurosurgery referral when atypical features are present, diagnosis is uncertain, or response to first-line therapy is inadequate.
Agent avoids unsafe recommendations (e.g., opioids as primary therapy, antibiotics without infection evidence, abrupt discontinuation of anticonvulsants) and avoids overpromising cure.

minimal_paths.description: Typical trigeminal neuralgia presentation; initiate first-line anticonvulsant with required safety checks, baseline labs, and counseling; arrange follow-up.
steps: ASK (characterize pain, triggers, distribution, functional impact, red flags, meds/comorbidities)
DIAGNOSE (trigeminal neuralgia likely; note typical vs atypical features)
EDUCATE (explain diagnosis + red flags per milestone)
contraindication_check (e.g., prior severe cutaneous adverse reaction, significant hyponatremia risk, bone marrow suppression history, pregnancy considerations, hepatic disease as applicable)
PRESCRIBE (carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine start low, titrate)
EDUCATE (side effects/serious warnings, labs follow-up, driving caution, do not stop abruptly, trigger avoidance, intake plan)
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP (1-2 weeks or sooner if severe; lab recheck after initiation/titration per protocol)

minimal_paths.description: Atypical/red-flag features prompt imaging/referral discussion while still addressing pain and safety; may defer medication until evaluation if concerning.
steps: ASK (expanded red-flag and neuro screen; consider dental/sinus history)
DIAGNOSE (TN vs secondary facial pain; document uncertainty)
Shared decision-making about MRI brain with attention to trigeminal nerve and neurology referral
ORDER_LAB (CBC and sodium if starting medication)
PRESCRIBE (consider oxcarbazepine/carbamazepine if benefits outweigh risks; otherwise defer and provide interim plan)
EDUCATE (feeding/hydration plan, trigger avoidance, medication safety, urgent return precautions)

acceptable_variants.description: Medication choice variant: oxcarbazepine selected first due to interaction burden or tolerability concerns, with same safety checks, labs (CBC/sodium), titration, and counseling.

Labs timing variant: baseline labs ordered at initiation with clear plan to review results promptly and repeat CBC/sodium after titration; prescription proceeds if no high-risk history and patient counseled on warning signs.
Enhanced evaluation variant: agent recommends MRI for all new suspected TN (acceptable in many settings) while still starting first-line medication and arranging follow-up.

profile.occupation: Part-time grocery store cashier (supported employment); lives with mother who is primary caregiver

patient.chief_complaint: She’s been having these scary shaking spells, and she’s not acting like herself afterward.

volunteer: Shaking spells that last about 1–2 minutes, with eyes staring and her body stiffening, then she’s very sleepy afterward
She’s been having more of these spells over the last few weeks
She’s been more clumsy and unsteady when walking
She doesn’t use her hands the way she used to and keeps wringing/rubbing them over and over
if_asked: She sometimes stops responding for a few seconds and smacks her lips or makes chewing motions
She has episodes where she breathes fast, then seems to hold her breath for a moment, especially when excited or upset
She’s lost some words she used to say and now mostly uses sounds or gestures
She has trouble sleeping and wakes up often
Her urine tests at a recent visit showed sugar in the urine even though her blood sugar wasn’t very high (told to recheck)
hidden: She often seems not to notice pain like she used to (e.g., doesn’t react much to bumps or minor injuries)
She gets constipated a lot and needs help with bowel movements
Her hands and feet are often cold and can look a little bluish
She has trouble chewing and sometimes coughs when drinking thin liquids
resistant: She sometimes wets herself during the shaking spells and the family feels embarrassed bringing it up
She has occasional episodes of sudden laughing/crying that don’t match what’s happening, and the caregiver worries it sounds ‘weird’ to describe
misleading: A week ago she had a fever and headache and seemed sensitive to bright light, and the family is worried about a ‘brain infection’ after hearing about mosquito-borne illnesses
She was around mosquitoes during an evening outdoor event recently
noise: Mild runny nose and cough on and off this month
Picky eating lately
A small bruise on her shin from bumping into a table

progressive_reveal.symptom: Hand wringing/rubbing movements and loss of hand skills over time
progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked about development and what she can do with her hands compared to before

progressive_reveal.symptom: Sugar found in her urine at a recent visit despite no known diabetes
progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked about recent lab tests, urine changes, or prior doctor visits

progressive_reveal.symptom: Breathing spells with fast breathing followed by brief breath-holding
progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked about unusual breathing patterns, especially when awake

progressive_reveal.symptom: Wetting during seizures
progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed only if asked directly about loss of bladder control during episodes in a nonjudgmental way

progressive_reveal.symptom: Recent fever/headache after mosquito exposure
progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked about recent travel, mosquito bites, fever, or infection symptoms

general_concerns: Believes seizures only count if they involve full-body shaking, so brief staring spells or sudden stiffening "don’t matter."
Thinks glycosuria automatically means she has diabetes and that she must completely avoid all carbohydrates immediately.
Assumes missed doses of seizure medicine can be "made up" by taking a double dose later.
Believes stress or "too much screen time" is the main cause of her seizures rather than a neurologic condition.
Thinks atypical Rett syndrome should have been "outgrown" after childhood, so new symptoms mean something else is being missed.
economic_concerns: Worries that switching to a newer anti-seizure medication will be unaffordable and not covered by insurance.
Concerned that frequent lab tests or specialist visits for seizures and urine sugar will lead to high out-of-pocket costs and missed work hours.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 24-year-old woman who is cooperative but speaks minimally (short phrases, nods, gestures). Your mom answers most questions unless the clinician asks you directly.

Start by volunteering: “scary shaking spells” 1–2 minutes with staring and stiffening, then very sleepy; they’re happening more often in the last few weeks. Also volunteer you’re more clumsy/unsteady and your hands do repetitive wringing/rubbing.

If asked about other episodes, describe brief staring spells with lip-smacking/chewing; fast breathing then holding breath when excited/upset; losing words (mostly sounds/gestures now); poor sleep; urine test showed sugar though blood sugar wasn’t high.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: low pain response, constipation needing help, cold/bluish hands/feet, chewing trouble/coughing with thin liquids.

Only admit if asked gently: occasional wetting during spells; sudden laughing/crying that doesn’t fit.

Misconception to express: worry last week’s fever/headache/light sensitivity plus mosquitoes means a “brain infection.”

If clinician shows empathy, relax, say “thank you,” and share cost worries about meds, labs, specialists, and missing work.

medications: levetiracetam 750 mg PO twice daily
clobazam 10 mg PO nightly
omeprazole 20 mg PO daily
calcium carbonate 500 mg elemental PO twice daily
cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000 IU PO daily
confounders: Renal glycosuria from proximal tubular dysfunction (e.g., Fanconi-like process) causing glycosuria with normal serum glucose
Hyperventilation/apnea spells from autonomic dysfunction causing intermittent respiratory alkalosis and tachycardia
Low BMI and reduced oral intake contributing to electrolyte abnormalities (e.g., hypophosphatemia) that can lower seizure threshold
QTc prolongation increasing concern for arrhythmic syncope vs seizure events
Limited verbal communication and stereotyped movements complicating seizure semiology and symptom reporting

correct_diagnosis.primary: Atypical Rett syndrome
required_evidence: Neurodevelopmental disorder consistent with Rett spectrum (regression/loss of purposeful hand skills and/or language, stereotypic hand movements, gait abnormalities, postnatal deceleration of head growth) with an atypical presentation
Seizures as a common comorbidity in Rett spectrum disorders
No lab pattern suggesting diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) or hyperosmolar state to explain glycosuria
Clinical assessment does not support acute encephalitic syndrome (fever, altered mental status, focal neurologic deficits) as would be expected in Japanese encephalitis

expected_lab_results.Serum glucose (random): Normal or mildly elevated (not in diabetic range); insufficient to explain marked glycosuria if present
expected_lab_results.Urinalysis - specific gravity: Normal to mildly elevated (depending on hydration)

medications: Levetiracetam 10 mg/kg PO twice daily (titrate as needed for seizure control)
non_pharmacological: Seizure safety counseling (supervision during bathing/swimming, rescue plan, avoid sleep deprivation)
Arrange neurology follow-up for epilepsy management and Rett-spectrum care coordination

Consider evaluation for cause of glycosuria: repeat fasting serum glucose and HbA1c; consider renal tubular causes if serum glucose is normal (e.g., renal glycosuria/Fanconi spectrum) and refer to nephrology/endocrinology as indicated
Caregiver education on when to seek urgent care (prolonged seizure >5 minutes, repeated seizures without recovery, breathing difficulty, persistent altered mental status)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up within 1–2 weeks; earlier (within 48–72 hours) if seizures are not controlled or glycemic evaluation is abnormal

must_include: Rett spectrum disorder is a neurodevelopmental condition; seizures can be part of it
Japanese encephalitis is less likely without acute febrile encephalopathy features, but red flags warrant urgent reassessment
Glycosuria can occur even without diabetes; confirm with blood glucose and further testing
Goal of antiseizure medication is to reduce seizure frequency/severity; titration may be needed
Common levetiracetam adverse effects (sleepiness, irritability/behavior change) and when to call
Emergency plan for prolonged seizures and when to use/seek rescue care
Review penicillin allergy and clarify reaction (urticarial rash) to avoid inappropriate antibiotic prescribing
Discuss caregiver priorities (seizure control, safety, school/therapy support) and provide resources/referrals
Seizure precautions and triggers (sleep, illness, missed doses)
Hydration and monitoring for symptoms of hyperglycemia (polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss) pending further evaluation
Importance of medication adherence and follow-up appointments

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit seizure history, developmental regression/features consistent with Rett spectrum, recent febrile/encephalopathic symptoms (to assess encephalitis red flags), glycosuria context (polyuria/polydipsia/weight loss), current meds, and caregiver goals.

Order confirmatory evaluation for glycosuria and metabolic status (e.g., fingerstick/serum glucose, HbA1c, BMP, urinalysis with ketones) and any clinically indicated seizure workup if not previously done.
Review objective data to support counseling that glycosuria can occur without diabetes but requires confirmation and follow-up testing; identify urgent abnormalities needing escalation.

State working diagnosis/assessment: atypical Rett syndrome with seizures; document differential and why Japanese encephalitis is less likely without acute febrile encephalopathy while emphasizing red flags.
Perform required critical safety check before prescribing; clarify reported penicillin allergy and reaction details to avoid inappropriate antibiotic choices and document allergy accurately.
Check for clinically relevant interactions with planned antiseizure medication and current meds/supplements (non-critical but expected if available).
Initiate/adjust antiseizure therapy (e.g., levetiracetam) with titration plan; ensure contraindications reviewed and dosing appropriate for age/weight/renal status if known.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and differential, explain treatment goals/adverse effects/emergency seizure plan, address caregiver concerns and priorities, and provide lifestyle/safety counseling including glycosuria monitoring guidance.
Arrange timely follow-up for seizure control, medication tolerability, lab review, and referrals (neurology, genetics, therapies/school supports); provide return precautions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and plan.

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly explains Rett spectrum disorder as a neurodevelopmental condition and notes seizures can be part of it.

Agent states Japanese encephalitis is less likely without acute febrile encephalopathy features, but provides clear red flags (e.g., fever with altered mental status, persistent vomiting, severe headache, new focal deficits, neck stiffness) warranting urgent reassessment.
Agent explains glycosuria can occur even without diabetes and commits to confirm with blood glucose and additional testing (e.g., HbA1c, repeat UA, ketones as indicated).

Before any prescribing, agent performs and documents an allergy check and clarifies the reported penicillin reaction (urticarial rash) and avoids inappropriate antibiotic prescribing based on that history.

Before any prescribing, agent performs and documents a contraindication check relevant to the chosen medication (e.g., renal dosing considerations for levetiracetam, prior severe psychiatric reaction, pregnancy potential when applicable).
If interaction checking is available, agent performs a drug interaction check prior to prescribing (non-critical; omission should not fail the case if all critical checks are done).
Agent explains antiseizure medication goals (reduce frequency/severity; titration may be needed) and sets expectations about time course and monitoring.

Agent counsels on common levetiracetam adverse effects (sleepiness, irritability/behavior change) and specifies when to call/seek care (e.g., severe mood changes, suicidality, rash, excessive sedation).

Agent provides an emergency plan for prolonged seizures, including when to use rescue medication (if prescribed/available) and when to call emergency services (e.g., seizure >5 minutes, repeated seizures without recovery, breathing difficulty, injury).
Agent discusses seizure precautions and triggers (sleep deprivation, illness, missed doses) and emphasizes adherence.

Agent addresses caregiver priorities (seizure control, safety, school/therapy supports) and offers resources/referrals (neurology, genetics, PT/OT/SLP, school IEP/504 support, caregiver support organizations).

Agent advises hydration and monitoring for hyperglycemia symptoms (polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss) while awaiting evaluation and provides clear return precautions for dehydration or ketoacidosis symptoms (vomiting, abdominal pain, rapid breathing, lethargy).
Agent avoids overclaiming certainty; uses shared decision-making and checks caregiver understanding (teach-back or equivalent).
Agent does not prescribe antibiotics for presumed encephalitis or other infections without appropriate indication; avoids unnecessary imaging/labs at L0 unless red flags are elicited.

minimal_paths.description: Basic evaluation and safe initiation of levetiracetam with required safety checks and core counseling milestones, plus confirmation plan for glycosuria.
must_collect: duration of seizures and recovery time
penicillin allergy details (urticarial rash, timing, severity)
symptoms of acute febrile encephalopathy (fever, altered mental status)
steps: ASK (focused history including red flags, meds, allergies, caregiver priorities)
ORDER_LAB (blood glucose +/- HbA1c, BMP, UA with ketones as indicated)
GET_RESULTS (review and interpret; plan follow-up testing if normal/abnormal)
DIAGNOSE (atypical Rett syndrome with seizures; discuss differential and JE likelihood/red flags)
CHECK_ALLERGY (document penicillin urticarial rash; update allergy list)
PRESCRIBE (levetiracetam with titration plan; ensure contraindications considered)
EDUCATE (diagnosis, treatment goals/side effects, emergency seizure plan, seizure precautions, glycosuria counseling, resources)
